quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Work by a variable force"?

options.text: 1 hp = 746 W; a unit of power commonly used for engines and motors
J = ∫F dt = Δp; the time integral of force equals the change in momentum
W = ∫F·dr; requires integration when force changes with position (e.g., spring force, gravitational force at large distances)
sinθ ≈ θ (in radians) for small angles; this linearizes the restoring torque and produces SHM

quiz_questions.explanation: "Work by a variable force" is defined as: W = ∫F·dr; requires integration when force changes with position (e.g., spring force, gravitational force at large distances). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Work by a spring"?

options.text: The pulling force transmitted through a string, rope, or cable; in an ideal (massless) string, tension is the same throughout
F_c = mv²/r; the net radial force needed to maintain circular motion; always directed toward the center
W = -½kx²₂ + ½kx²₁; found by integrating F = -kx from position x₁ to x₂
F_ext = dp/dt applied to rockets: thrust = v_exhaust × |dm/dt|, where mass decreases as fuel is expelled

quiz_questions.explanation: "Work by a spring" is defined as: W = -½kx²₂ + ½kx²₁; found by integrating F = -kx from position x₁ to x₂. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Hooke's Law"?

options.text: F = -kx; the restoring force of an ideal spring is proportional to displacement from equilibrium and directed opposite to it
J = ∫F dt = Δp; the integral of force over time equals the change in momentum; measured in N·s
F_c = mv²/r; the net radial force needed to maintain circular motion; always directed toward the center
f_s ≤ μ_s N; opposes the tendency of motion between surfaces in contact; self-adjusting up to the maximum value μ_s N

quiz_questions.explanation: "Hooke's Law" is defined as: F = -kx; the restoring force of an ideal spring is proportional to displacement from equilibrium and directed opposite to it. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Work and Kinetic Energy, which best defines "Kinetic energy"?

options.text: ∫τ dt = ΔL; the angular analog of linear impulse equals the change in angular momentum
A dimensionless vector of magnitude 1 indicating direction; î, ĵ, k̂ denote the x, y, z directions respectively
ω = √(k/m) = 2πf = 2π/T; determines how fast the oscillation occurs
KE = ½mv²; the energy associated with an object's motion; always non-negative

quiz_questions.explanation: In Work and Kinetic Energy, "Kinetic energy" refers to: KE = ½mv²; the energy associated with an object's motion; always non-negative.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Work and Kinetic Energy, which best defines "Work-Energy Theorem"?

options.text: f_s ≤ μ_s N; opposes the tendency of motion between surfaces in contact; self-adjusting up to the maximum value μ_s N
v(t) = dx/dt; the derivative of position with respect to time, giving the rate and direction of motion at each instant
W_net = ½mv² - ½mv₀²; relates the total work done by all forces to the change in kinetic energy
W_nc = ΔKE + ΔPE = E_final - E_initial; work by friction or applied forces that changes the total mechanical energy

quiz_questions.explanation: In Work and Kinetic Energy, "Work-Energy Theorem" refers to: W_net = ½mv² - ½mv₀²; relates the total work done by all forces to the change in kinetic energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "W = ∫F·dr; requires integration when force changes with position (e.g., spring force, gravitational force at large distances)"?

options.text: Torque-angular momentum relation
Work by a variable force
Unit vector
Normal force on incline

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Work by a variable force".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "W = -½kx²₂ + ½kx²₁; found by integrating F = -kx from position x₁ to x₂"?

options.text: Angular frequency
Work by a spring
Cross product
Average power

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Work by a spring".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "F = -kx; the restoring force of an ideal spring is proportional to displacement from equilibrium and directed opposite to it"?

options.text: Hooke's Law
Position from velocity
Kepler's Third Law
Conservation of mechanical energy

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Hooke's Law".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Work and Kinetic Energy, which term matches: "KE = ½mv²; the energy associated with an object's motion; always non-negative"?

options.text: Kinetic energy
Non-uniform circular motion
Instantaneous power
Uniform circular motion

quiz_questions.explanation: In Work and Kinetic Energy, this refers to "Kinetic energy".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Work and Kinetic Energy, which term matches: "W_net = ½mv² - ½mv₀²; relates the total work done by all forces to the change in kinetic energy"?

options.text: Restoring torque
Work-Energy Theorem
Non-uniform circular motion
Static equilibrium

quiz_questions.explanation: In Work and Kinetic Energy, this refers to "Work-Energy Theorem".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Work and Kinetic Energy is accurate?

options.text: For variable forces, the impulse is the area under the force-time graph.
Wave properties—wavelength λ, frequency f, speed v = λf, and amplitude—are described mathematically by y(x,t) = A sin(kx - ωt), linking oscillation parameters to wave propagation.

Conservation of momentum applies to collisions, explosions, and recoil in one and two dimensions, and is particularly powerful when forces are impulsive (large and brief) or when the system is isolated.
Work and Kinetic Energy defines work rigorously using calculus: W = ∫F·dr, the line integral of force along the displacement path.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Work and Kinetic Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Work and Kinetic Energy?

options.text: Multiple-force problems require careful free-body analysis at specific positions.

Energy conservation with U = -GMm/r is essential for orbit analysis: total mechanical energy E = ½mv² - GMm/r determines whether an orbit is bound (E < 0) or unbound (E ≥ 0), and escape velocity follows from setting E = 0.
The angular frequency ω = √(k/m) depends on system parameters (spring constant and mass), not amplitude.
The Work-Energy Theorem (W_net = ΔKE) provides a powerful alternative to Newton's Second Law for solving problems.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Work and Kinetic Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Work and Kinetic Energy?

options.text: Work is defined as W = ∫F·dr (the dot product integral of force along the displacement path), and the Work-Energy Theorem states W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mv₀².
Newton's Second Law in its most general form states F_net = dp/dt, which reduces to F = ma when mass is constant.
Momentum and Impulse are deeply connected: the impulse (J = ∫F dt) delivered to an object equals its change in momentum (Δp = mΔv).
For variable forces (such as springs: F = -kx), integration is essential: W_spring = ∫(-kx)dx = -½kx².

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Work and Kinetic Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Work and Kinetic Energy?

options.text: For constant angular acceleration, the rotational kinematic equations parallel their linear counterparts.
The gravitational field g = GM/r² at distance r from a mass M gives the local acceleration due to gravity, explaining why g varies with altitude and location on Earth.
AP Physics C emphasizes setting up and evaluating these integrals.
Systematic problem-solving involves drawing free-body diagrams, choosing a coordinate system, applying ΣF = ma in each direction, and solving the resulting system of equations.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Work and Kinetic Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Work and Kinetic Energy?

options.text: Newton's Second Law for rotation in momentum form gives τ_net = dL/dt.
Work and Kinetic Energy defines work rigorously using calculus: W = ∫F·dr, the line integral of force along the displacement path.
The gravitational field g = GM/r² at distance r from a mass M gives the local acceleration due to gravity, explaining why g varies with altitude and location on Earth.
The normal force N adjusts to maintain contact and is not always equal to mg—it depends on the geometry and other applied forces.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Work and Kinetic Energy as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Work and Kinetic Energy?

options.text: Automotive suspension system analysis modeling shock absorbers as damped SHM to optimize ride comfort and handling characteristics
Engineering spring-loaded mechanisms (car bumpers, mattresses) by calculating work stored and released using W = ½kx²
Hard drive engineering specifying angular velocity (7200 RPM) and computing read/write head linear speed at different radii
Rocket propulsion using F = dp/dt with changing mass: thrust = (dm/dt)·v_exhaust, relating exhaust velocity and fuel burn rate to propulsive force

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Work and Kinetic Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Work and Kinetic Energy?

options.text: Calculating the work done by a non-constant wind force on a sail using numerical integration of force-displacement data
Pole vaulting biomechanics analyzing the conversion of kinetic energy → elastic PE in the pole → gravitational PE at peak height
Designing anti-lock braking systems (ABS) that maintain static friction (which exceeds kinetic) by preventing wheel lockup during emergency stops
Amusement park ride engineering ensuring loop-the-loop roller coasters maintain sufficient speed at the top for the normal force to remain positive

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Work and Kinetic Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Work and Kinetic Energy applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Analyzing the work required to stretch a bungee cord by integrating the non-linear elastic force over the extension distance
Amusement park ride engineering ensuring loop-the-loop roller coasters maintain sufficient speed at the top for the normal force to remain positive
High jump technique (Fosbury Flop) allows the athlete's center of mass to pass below the bar even as their body clears it
Designing anti-lock braking systems (ABS) that maintain static friction (which exceeds kinetic) by preventing wheel lockup during emergency stops

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Work and Kinetic Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Work and Kinetic Energy?

options.text: Engineering spring-loaded mechanisms (car bumpers, mattresses) by calculating work stored and released using W = ½kx²
Rocket propulsion using F = dp/dt with changing mass: thrust = (dm/dt)·v_exhaust, relating exhaust velocity and fuel burn rate to propulsive force
Hydroelectric dam design converting gravitational PE of water (mgh) to kinetic energy of turbines to electrical energy, analyzing efficiency at each conversion stage
Designing pulley systems (block and tackle) to provide mechanical advantage for lifting heavy loads in construction

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Work and Kinetic Energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Work and Kinetic Energy in practice?

options.text: Forensic physics reconstructing car accident speeds from skid mark lengths using W_friction = ΔKE to calculate the initial velocity
Flywheel energy storage systems using high-I, high-ω wheels to store kinetic energy for regenerative braking in vehicles and power grid stabilization
Calculating the work done by a non-constant wind force on a sail using numerical integration of force-displacement data
Figure skating spins: pulling arms in reduces I, increasing ω to conserve angular momentum (demonstrating I's physical significance)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Work and Kinetic Energy.
